domain:
A Person has a name and an identifier. Every Person is the child of another Person and can have an indeterminate number of children (however, there will be one Person who is not the child of any other Person). A Person has an indeterminate number of ancestors (the ancestors of a Person are their father and the ancestors of their father). We will be interested in knowing the degree of each ancestor of a Person (1 for the father, 2 for the father's father, etc.).

A Resource has a name. There are two types of Resources, such as Files and Folders. Every resource belongs to a Folder and cannot exist outside of a Folder (however, there will be one Folder that does not follow that rule, the root Folder does not belong to any other Folder). Each Folder contains an indeterminate number of Resources.

A User is a Person who, in addition to a name and an identifier, also has a password to access an indeterminate number of Resources, and each Resource can be accessed by an indeterminate number of Users. When a User can access a resource, we will be interested in knowing if they can view it and if they can edit it.

questions.question:
Is ancestor a relevant domain concept that should be included in the model?

For example:
1. Ancestor is a relevant concept in this domain and should be represented in the model.
2. Ancestor does not correspond to any relevant domain concept and should be removed from the model.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Keep ancestor, it is a relevant domain concept.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Remove ancestor, it is not relevant.
questions.selected: Option 1: Keep ancestor, it is a relevant domain concept.

questions.question:
Should ancestor be modeled as a separate class associated with Person, or as a simple representation within Person?

For example:
1. Ancestor is a separate class with attributes: degree. This allows users to query and filter by individual ancestor properties.
2. Ancestor is modeled as a simple represntation in Person. This is suitable when ancestor details are not needed.
Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: The Ancestor is a separate class with its own attributes.
questions.option_2: Option 2: The ancestor is a simple representation in Person.
questions.selected: Option 1: The Ancestor is a separate class with its own attributes.

questions.question:
Should the relationship between Person and Ancestor be modeled as a composition or as an association?

For example:
1. Use composition, Ancestor would depend on Person for its existence.
2. Use association, Ancestor could exist independently of Person, and can also be linked to multiple people.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Composition is needed because class Ancestor depends on Person.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Association is sufficient because class Ancestor exists independently of Person.
questions.selected: Option 2: Association is sufficient because class Ancestor exists independently of Person.
questions.llm_raw_response:
Option 2: Association is sufficient because class Ancestor exists independently of Person.

Ancestors are themselves Persons (a Person can be the father/ancestor of multiple other Persons) and do not depend on a particular descendant for their existence.

questions.question:
What is the minimum cardinality of Person in the relationship with Ancestor?

For example:
1. Minimum cardinality of zero, an ancestor can exist without any associated person.
2. Minimum cardinality of one, an ancestor must have at least one associated person.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Ancestor can exist without Person.
questions.option_2: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality,Ancestor must have at least one Person.
questions.selected: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality,Ancestor must have at least one Person.

questions.question:
What is the minimum cardinality of Ancestor in the relationship with Person?

For example:
1. Minimum cardinality of zero, a person can exist without any associated ancestor.
2. Minimum cardinality of one, a person must have at least one associated ancestor.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Person can exist without Ancestor.
questions.option_2: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality,Person must have at least one Ancestor.
questions.selected: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Person can exist without Ancestor.

questions.question:
What is the maximum cardinality of Ancestor in the relationship with Person?

For example:
1. Maximum cardinality of one, a person is associated with exactly one ancestor.
2. Maximum cardinality of many, a person can be associated with multiple ancestors.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: One as maximun cardinality, each person links to one ancestor.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each person can link to multiple ancestors.
questions.selected: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each person can link to multiple ancestors.

questions.question:
What is the minimum cardinality of User in the relationship with Access?

For example:
1. Minimum cardinality of zero, an access can exist without any associated user.
2. Minimum cardinality of one, an access must have at least one associated user.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Access can exist without User.
questions.option_2: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality,Access must have at least one User.
questions.selected: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality,Access must have at least one User.

questions.question:
What is the maximum cardinality of User in the relationship with Access?

For example:
1. Maximum cardinality of one, an access is associated with exactly one user.
2. Maximum cardinality of many, an access can be associated with multiple users.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: One as maximun cardinality, each access links to one user.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each access can link to multiple users.
questions.selected: Option 1: One as maximun cardinality, each access links to one user.

questions.question:
What is the minimum cardinality of Access in the relationship with User?

For example:
1. Minimum cardinality of zero, a user can exist without any associated access.
2. Minimum cardinality of one, a user must have at least one associated access.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, User can exist without Access.
questions.option_2: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality, User must have at least one Access.
questions.selected: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality, User must have at least one Access.

questions.question:
What is the maximum cardinality of Access in the relationship with User?

For example:
1. Maximum cardinality of one, a user is associated with exactly one access.
2. Maximum cardinality of many, a user can be associated with multiple accesses.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: One as maximun cardinality, each user links to one access.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each user can link to multiple accesses.
questions.selected: Option 1: One as maximun cardinality, each user links to one access.

questions.question:
What is the maximum cardinality of Access in the relationship with Resource?

For example:
1. Maximum cardinality of one, a resource is associated with exactly one access.
2. Maximum cardinality of many, a resource can be associated with multiple accesses.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: One as maximun cardinality, each resource links to one access.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each resource can link to multiple accesses.
questions.selected: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each resource can link to multiple accesses.

questions.question:
What is the minimum cardinality of Resource in the relationship with Access?

For example:
1. Minimum cardinality of zero, an access can exist without any associated resource.
2. Minimum cardinality of one, an access must have at least one associated resource.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Access can exist without Resource.
questions.option_2: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality,Access must have at least one Resource.
questions.selected: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Access can exist without Resource.

questions.question:
What is the maximum cardinality of Resource in the relationship with Access?

For example:
1. Maximum cardinality of one, an access is associated with exactly one resource.
2. Maximum cardinality of many, an access can be associated with multiple resources.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: One as maximun cardinality, each access links to one resource.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each access can link to multiple resources.
questions.selected: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each access can link to multiple resources.

questions.question:
Should the relationship between Folder and Resource be modeled as a composition or as an association?

For example:
1. Use composition, Resource would depend on Folder for its existence.
2. Use association, Resource could exist independently of Folder, and can also be linked to multiple folders.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Composition is needed because class Resource depends on Folder.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Association is sufficient because class Resource exists independently of Folder.
questions.selected: Option 1: Composition is needed because class Resource depends on Folder.

questions.question:
Should Access be modeled as an association class linking User and Resource, or as a regular class with two separate associations?

For example:
1. Association class: Access exists only as part of the relationship between User and Resource. A access cannot exist without the link between User and Resource.
2. Regular class: Access is a class that has links to User and Resource. In this case multiple accesses can exist for every User and Resource.


Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Association class, Access as association class linking User and Resource.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Regular class, Access as separate class with two associations.
questions.selected: Option 1: Association class, Access as association class linking User and Resource.
